dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond for an adult male with breathing problems at Chippenham Hospital.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
It's been hard to breathe for about a week. Recently got worse, and the albuterol I have isn't helping now.
Mr. Henry, what is your full name for documentation?
Henry Clark.
Where are we now, Mr. Henry?
In Richmond.
Airway is patent and unobstructed. No foreign bodies or secretions noted.
You're using tripod posture and accessory muscles for breathing. Are you experiencing pain with that?
No pain. It's just tough to get air.
Your respiratory rate is 35. The chest rises and falls symmetrically with no cyanosis noted.
Can we take your blood pressure?
Blood pressure is 160 over 88.
Heart rate is 86. Pulse is strong and regular in both arms.
I'll put this non-rebreather on. What's your SpO2 on room air?
It reads 96%.
You mentioned five uses of albuterol today. Is it working at all?
It used to work. Now no, it doesn't help no more.
Did you receive a similar diagnosis at the hospital four days ago?
Yes, they sent me home with prescriptions for albuterol, Atrovent, Azithromycin, and Prednisone.
Have you filled those prescriptions yet?
No, I haven't. The albuterol I have just doesn't help now.
Are you taking any other medicines now?
I take a lot of different pills, but I can't remember the names.
Any known drug or food allergies?
No known drug or food allergies.
What did you have to eat today?
Orange juice around 11. I haven't had anything since then.
How would you rate your breathing today? On a scale of 0 to 10?
It's about a 3 or 4. Not pain, but really labored.
I notice fast and shallow breathing with grunting. Is this normal?
Yeah, that's how it's always been.
Radial pulses are strong and equal. Skin is warm and dry. Your color looks good.
No signs of jugular venous distension seen. No bleeding noted.
Your pupils are equal and reactive-both at 3mm. No excess discharge or secretions.
You look agitated with spasms in your face and arms. Has that always been the case?
Yes, that's how I've always been. I just cope with it.
Breathing is symmetrical with diminished air movement in both fields.
Chest shows normal rise and fall with no pain or deformity. EKG not yet complete.
Skin is warm and unbroken with no rashes, scars or bites noted.
No signs of discoloration, trauma, or DCAP-BTLS on the torso or neck.
Pos PMS bilaterally. Arms and legs have normal strength and coordination.
4-lead ECG applied for cardiac monitoring.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with occasional PVCs. No signs of ventricular bigeminy.
Let's check your blood sugar with a quick finger stick.
There's no reading displayed for your glucose-monitor can't detect a value currently.
We're starting capnography to track your CO2 levels.
Capnography shows a mild sharkfin waveform with no hypercarbia-looks stable for now.
Terbutaline is being administered via subcutaneous injection to help with bronchospasm.
IV access in the left antecubital was attempted, but it failed due to involuntary muscle movements.
We tried IM dexamethasone but you pulled away from the needle.
Breathing is 24 per minute now. No pain reported. SpO2 increased to 98%.
Blood pressure has risen slightly to 176 over 108. Monitoring continues.
Retried IM dexamethasone but you again refused the injection.
Breathing is still labored but SpO2 and vitals are in the acceptable range.
Any new symptoms like fever, nausea, or chest discomfort now?
No, it's still just hard to get air.
Any recent sore throat or infection that might be causing this?
I haven't had any illness recently.
Pulse is 79. EKG now shows a return to sinus rhythm without PVCs.
SpO2 is at 92%. It dropped but remains acceptable for now.
Vital signs are stable, but your breathing remains labored.
No major complications. Repeating monitoring as we proceed with transport.
A 12-lead ECG has been obtained, showing a sinus rhythm with occasional PVCs.
This is a respiratory distress/asthma protocol under the Standing Order 3-11.
Fowler's position for you to assist with breathing. You'll be secured with lap and shoulder belts.
You're heading to Chippenham for advanced care. We've already called ahead.
The hospital is aware and room 17 is waiting for your arrival with our report.
Moving you now for safe transport up onto the stretcher. Try to stay as straight as you can.
You're on ongoing cardiac and capnography monitoring. If you can, just breathe slowly.
Vitals are good, and oxygen levels are improved. If things get worse, let us know.
ED knows you're coming in. Expecting evaluation for possible nebulizer or additional meds.
You're now fully secured. Let us know immediately if you feel any large changes.
Oxygen and monitors are set. Just try to rest and keep breathing.
We've been en route a bit over two minutes and vitals remain within normal limits.
Chippenham has been updated. Mr. Henry remains alert, with no improvement from albuterol efforts.
No signs of overt infection noted, but shortness of breath may stem from this.
Estimated arrival is within three minutes. Let's keep monitoring during transport.
No tenderness or abdominal guarding found. Discomfort isn't part of the current picture.
We have finished transport. ED is on standby, and all findings have been relayed.
All previous procedures, assessments, and interventions are now with the hospital team.
Limbs and body are free of injury, deformity, or wounds ID'd during exam.
No ACUTE or notable abnormal findings beyond the baseline asthmatic presentation.
Final blood pressure is 123 over 71, down from earlier values.
You received 0.25mg of terbutaline via subcutaneous injection to aid breathing.
You rate shortness of breath as 3 or 4. Concern for mild respiratory distress.
You are alert, oriented to time, place, and person. GCS is 15, A/O4/4.
You mention mild asthma as a known condition. Any flare-ups recently?
Yeah, this has been a flare-up for a few days now.
Airway is patent, with GCS 15 and A/O4/4. No signs of infection or jugular venous distension at this time.
I don't see any signs of jugular venous distension, and the neck veins look flat for now.
Skin turgor is normal, and I don't see any edema or swelling. Capillary refill is less than two seconds, as noted.
Shortness of breath is the main issue right now. Just keep breathing, we're just a few minutes out.
You're breathing through the non-rebreather. More oxygen may help ease the breathing rate a bit.
All findings including vital signs and exam have been communicated to the receiving hospital for a smooth transition of care.
Any dizziness, chest tightness, or cough along with the shortness of breath?
A bit of a cough, but no chest tightness.
No signs of infection at this point. No rashes or open wounds on skin. Skin is warm and unbroken.
12-lead ECG has been recorded, confirming a sinus rhythm with PVCs. That was completed earlier before transport preparation began.
All other essential details including labs, medications, and ECG findings have been communicated to the hospital for prompt care and further assessment.
